title: Lesson 356
transcript: ðŸŽµ ðŸŽµ Okay, we're in domain number two, of course, and it's focused on asset security, how we protect our assets. In this module, we're going to focus on specifically data as an example of an asset, that needs to be protected as it goes through its life cycle. So all of you have heard of the data life cycle, for example. And so we're going to focus on obviously how we protect things as they go through their life cycle phases from creation, for example, all the way to destruction. It requires us to focus on protecting based on that value before we get to the actual data life cycle itself. and how security fits in. We'll just remind you about some of the roles that any piece of data, for example, such an asset might be handled by. And we've talked about this before, but it's worth repeating and relate, for example, data security or asset security based on whether certain roles are accountable or responsible. And I think this is probably pretty evident because we've stressed it so many times in this course, but obviously the owner is always accountable. And we've also got controlling here because that is a role that is specified through laws and regulations that focus on privacy. In fact, a lot of privacy laws will require organizations that are collecting information on their clients and customers and others to establish a role within the organization called controller that automatically becomes accountable for protecting the information that you are collecting on your clients and customers that may relate to privacy. So on the exam, because the industry uses this terminology as well, if you see owner or the word controller, you can associate accountability for protecting information to those roles. Everything else that you see here, for example, data processors, that would be your IT function, for example. They are going to be responsible, of course, for anything related to security. your custodians, which may be, for example, system administrators and data administrators and so on, database administrators. are also going to be responsible for certain aspects of security as that information goes through its lifecycle. Data stewards, for example, are responsible for how that data is structured in different formats and architectures. throughout the organization as well, and they would be also responsible. And if you see the words data subject, of course that relates to the people that you are collecting information in regards to. And of course, you have the accountability to protect that information that ultimately belongs to your subjects, your clients, your customers, and others that you are collecting information in regards to. Now, here's that data lifecycle. And of course, even though it says data lifecycle here, this entire domain is focused on asset protection or asset security. We're just using data as an example here, but specifically because in the exam outline, they specifically mention the data lifecycle. So we're going to relate to data as an example of an asset that obviously needs to be protected as it goes through its lifecycle phases. And as you know, the first phase of any data lifecycle is going to be the create phase, sometimes also referred to as the collection phase or whatever. Obviously, we call it create and then we may share that data and we may store that data, for example, and we may archive it based on retention requirements that may be dictated by, for example, laws and regulations. And of course, the end of any asset lifecycle, including data, is going to be destruction. And so one of the themes that definitely appears on the exam is, first of all, that as any asset, including data, goes through its lifecycle, We have to protect it as it goes through its life cycle. Every phase will require protection based on the value of that asset. But it needs to start right at the create phase. so there may be questions on the exam that say you know when should data or any asset be classified and that is right at the create phase because again classification systems are there to afford assets the level of protection that they require based on their value. And so as soon as an asset is created, like data for example, it needs to be classified by the owners and then of course that ensures that we can start protecting it from that phase onward all the way to its destruction phase. Thank you.
